coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional phthalocyanine NIR sensor for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-imaging and removal of intracellular and environmental Cu2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xianglei
Ma, Gaoqiang
Zhang, Wei
Zhao, Wanru
Lian, Guixue
Zhang, Sen
Zhang, Dongjiao
Liu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Cu2+ sensing and removal are of great significance for environment protection and human health. Herein, multifunctional Cu2+ sensor system for both detection and removal of intracellular and environmental Cu2+ based on trans/cis interconversion of silicon phthalocyanine (SiPc) was developed. This phthalocyanine based Cu2+ sensor shows remarkably dual-mode NIR fluorescence and colorimetric responses for recognition of Cu2+, resulting in insoluble chelate for Cu2+ removal as well as the extremely high anti-interference in the presence of various anions and cations. The binding mechanism and stoichiometry ratio were systematically revealed. Intracellular fluorescence imaging in MCF-7 cells demonstrated that Cu2+ could be efficiently detected in living cells and form insoluble chelate for clearance. As the result, this novel phthalocyanine based multifunctional sensor can be used for dual-mode NIR fluorescence/colorimetric detection and removal of Cu2+ in either complicated environment or biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NIR sensor
Cu2+ detection/removal
Configuration interconversion
Dual-mode sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination binding stoichiometry of SiPc-NH2 to Cu2+
Cells culture and intracellular fluorescence detection of Cu2+
Binding mechanism of SiPc-NH2 to Cu2+
Cu2+ readout and removal in solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular structure and configurations of SiPc-NH2 and SiPc-NH2-CuCl2.
(a) Comparison of 1H NMR spectra of SiPc-NH2 in CDCl3 and CDCl3 with D2O (the solvent peaks were omitted for clarity). (b) MALDI-TOF mass spectra of SiPc-NH2 in CDCl3 and (c) in CDCl3 with D2O.

(a) The photographs for color changes under natural light or (b) UV lamp (365 nm) of SiPc-NH2 in the presence of various metal ions. (c) The fluorescence emission spectra and (d) the corresponding histogram, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities after and before adding different anions. (e) The absorption spectra and (f) the corresponding histogram, where A and A0 are the absorption after and before adding different anion. (g) Metal-ion competition histogram of Cu2+ to various of Mn+.

(a) The fluorescence intensity changes and (b) the corresponding histogram of SiPc-NH2 along with titration by CuCl2. (c) The linear fitting between CuCl2 concentrations and F0/F of SiPc-NH2, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities after and before adding CuCl2. (d) The comparison of colorimetric and fluorescent photo-images under natural light and UV lamp, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of SiPc-NH2 by adding different Cu2+ salts (5 equiv.) and (b) the corresponding histogram, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities after and before adding copper salts (inset are photographs of fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp).

(a) The photographs for color changes under natural light or (b) UV lamp (365 nm) of SiPc-NH2 (2 μM) in the presence of Cu(NO3)2 (10 μM) and various sodium salts (10 μM). (c) The fluorescence emission spectra (d) the corresponding histogram, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities after and before adding different anions. (e) The absorption spectra and (f) the corresponding histogram, where A and A0 are the absorption after and before adding different anion.
Illustration of binding ratio for SiPc-NH2 to Cu2+.

(a) The photographs of color changes under natural light and (b) UV lamp (365 nm) of SiPc-NH2 after titration with CuCl2 (0–2.0 equiv.). (c) The fluorescence intensity changes and (d) the corresponding histogram along with titration by CuCl2. (e) The absorption intensity changes and (f) the corresponding histogram along with titration by CuCl2.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XPS spectra of SiPc-NH2 and SiPc-NH2-CuCl2. (c) ESI-MS mass spectrum of SiPc-NH2-CuCl2.
High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (a), N 1 s (c), O 1 s (e) of SiPc-NH2 and C 1 s (b), N 1 s (d), O 1 s (f) of SiPc-NH2-CuCl2.

(a) Fluorescence and (b) absorption changes of SiPc-NH2 for detecting Cu2+ with excess of NaCl. (c) Fluorescence and (d) absorption changes of SiPc-NH2 for detecting Cl- with excess of CuNO3 (inset are photographs of color changes under 365 nm UV lamp (left) and natural light (right)).
(a)Confocal fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells after being incubated with medium (control), SiPc-NH2 and SiPc-NH2 + CuCl2. (b) Cell viability after 24 h treatment.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional phthalocyanine NIR sensor for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-imaging and removal of intracellular and environmental Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Crystal Materials, State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Crystal Materials, State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Implantology, School and Hospital of Stomatology, Cheeloo College of Medicine, Shandong University & Shandong Key Laboratory of Oral Tissue Regeneration & Shandong Engineering Laboratory for Dental Materials and Oral Tissue Regeneration & Shandong Provincial Clinical Research Center for Oral Diseases, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong University & Shandong Key Laboratory of Oral Tissue Regeneration & Shandong Engineering Laboratory for Dental Materials and Oral Tissue Regeneration & Shandong Provincial Clinical Research Center for Oral Diseases

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Implantology, School and Hospital of Stomatology, Cheeloo College of Medicine, Shandong University & Shandong Key Laboratory of Oral Tissue Regeneration & Shandong Engineering Laboratory for Dental Materials and Oral Tissue Regeneration & Shandong Provincial Clinical Research Center for Oral Diseases, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cu2+ sensing and removal are of great significance for environment protection and human health. Herein, multifunctional Cu2+ sensor system for both detection and removal of intracellular and environmental Cu2+ based on trans/cis interconversion of silicon phthalocyanine (SiPc) was developed. This phthalocyanine based Cu2+ sensor shows remarkably dual-mode NIR fluorescence and colorimetric responses for recognition of Cu2+, resulting in insoluble chelate for Cu2+ removal as well as the extremely high anti-interference in the presence of various anions and cations. The binding mechanism and stoichiometry ratio were systematically revealed. Intracellular fluorescence imaging in MCF-7 cells demonstrated that Cu2+ could be efficiently detected in living cells and form insoluble chelate for clearance. As the result, this novel phthalocyanine based multifunctional sensor can be used for dual-mode NIR fluorescence/colorimetric detection and removal of Cu2+ in either complicated environment or biological systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an essential element required for cofactors and enzymes during metabolic process for human life, copper ion (Cu2+) plays a significant role to the health of human body [1,2]. However, its concentration is immediately relating the health of human life, that is, either insufficient or excessive of copper could cause serious abnormal spoilages of organ [3]. For example, insufficient of copper could result leukopenia or anemia [4], in contrast, excessive copper may cause tumor [5,6], Alzheimer’s disease [7–10], Parkinson’s disease [11–14], etc. More seriously, it could not be biodegraded and would gradually accumulate in living organism as well as environment. However, penicillamine (PA), the most clinical used Cu2+antidote for treatment copper related disorder, cannot be used in patients with penicillin allergy and is not easily detected. In addition, PA overdose could induce various side-effects prejudicial to health and need to be monitored by complicated strategies. Therefore, the development of low cost, highly-sensitive and multi-functional sensor for both Cu2+ readout and removal are of particularly important to prevent pollution and ensure human health [15,16]. In fact, the material used to remove Cu2+ is far less than that used for Cu2+detecting. Therefore, multifunctional sensors that can accurately detect and remove Cu2+ are particularly important, especially for biological systems.

In recent decades, a variety of Cu2+ detection methods have been established, such as surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [17,18], fluorescence sensor [19–25], plasma mass spectroscopy [26], semiconductor electrochemical method [27–30] and colorimetric sensors [31–33] etc. Among these technologies, either fluorescence or colorimetric method has attracted increasing attention due to their intuitive visibility, intrinsic ease of operation and high sensitivity. Therefore, the combinational fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensor will greatly improve the detection accuracy and sensitivity. In particular, the NIR fluorescence or colorimetric probes are more attractive due to their deep tissue penetration as well as avoiding self-interference from autofluorescence of tissues [15]. So far, a series of fluorescence materials have been developed for Cu2+ detection, including modified carbon dots [34,35], AIE fluorescence molecules [36], MOF [37,38], semiconductor nanoparticle [39] or polymer assembled nanoparticle [40–42] etc. Unfortunately, these nanoparticles or MOF materials are easily dissociated in physiological or complicated solutions, reducing their sensitivity and accuracy. Hence, small molecular sensors with high stability and sensitivity for unique recognition and imaging Cu2+ are more desirable.

Phthalocyanine (Pc) is a kind of macrocyclic molecule with 18π-electron-conjugated planar structure, enabling it with distinctive photophysical and photochemical properties in the red to NIR region. Their outstanding photo- and thermo-stability and low toxicity render them widely investigated over a range of catalysts [43], photo-conducting agents [44], gas sensors [45,46], optical storage materials [47], photovoltaic cells [48]. However, except the aggregation indued florescence quenching, it is not easy to change either colorimetric nor fluorescence signal of phthalocyanines due to their rigid planar structure and excellent stability. Thus, the majority of applications are mainly achieved by detecting their electrical signals using sophisticated instruments. To the best of our knowledge, phthalocyanine based fluorescent or colorimetric sensors have been rarely reported so far.

It is well known that the configuration of a molecule greatly affects its properties and application. For examples, the widely used broad-spectrum anticancer drug of dichloro-diamine platinum is only effective in its cis-configuration, namely cisplatin, which has excellent treatment efficacy on ovarian cancer, prostate cancer, testicular cancer, lung cancer, nasopharyngeal cancer, esophageal cancer et al. [49]. The cis–trans isomerization of unsaturated lipids play an important role during radical attack induced cellular damage since it determines the barrier properties and functions of biological membranes [50]. The different cis/trans-proline states are associated with tumorigenesis [51] and the formation of neurodegenerative diseases [52] and circadian rhythm regulation [53]. As the result, configuration changes in molecules play a decisive role in their physical, chemical and biological properties, leading to different applications.

Herein, phthalocyanine based multifunctional sensor with dual-mode NIR fluorescence and colorimetric readout for selective detecting and removal Cu2+ was developed through coordination induced configuration interconversion from trans- to cis-SiPc-NH2 (Fig. 1 ). It exhibited extremely high sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ recognition at the presence of Cl- along with remarkable colorimetric and fluorescence response, resulting in insoluble chelate for copper removal as well as high anti-interference in the coexistence of other metal ions and anions. The Cu2+ recognition and binding mechanism are systematically revealed with a 1:1 binding ratio. Intracellular fluorescence imaging in MCF-7 cells demonstrated that Cu2+ could be efficiently recognized in living cell and form insoluble chelate for removal, making it a multifunctional molecular antidote for both readout and removal Cu2+ in either biological or complicated environmental systems.

Silicon phthalocyanine dichloride (SiPcCl2), 2-(2-aminoethoxy)ethanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Metal salts: AlCl3, BaCl2, CaCl2, CdCl2, CeCl3, CoCl2, CrCl3, CuCl2, FeCl3, KCl, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaCl, NiCl2, PbCl2, ZnCl2, Cu(OAc)2, CuBr2, Cu(ClO4)2, Cu(NO3)2, CuSO4, NaNO3, NaAcO, NaBr, Na2CO3, NaF, NaHCO3, Na2HPO4, NaI, NaOH, Na2S, Na2SO4, Al(NO3)3, Ba(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Ce(NO3)4, Co(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)3, Mg(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2 and Zn(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2 and AgNO3 were purchased Macklin and used as received. All of the other reagents were of analytical grade without further treatment. The silicon phthalocyanine SiPc-NH2 was synthesized according to the reported procedure [54].

1H NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker AVANCE 300 MHz spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer Lambda-35 UV–vis spectrometer. Steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-2700 spectrofluorimeter equipped with a 450 W Xe lamp. FT-IR spectra were acquired on a Nicolet NEXUS 670 Fourier transformation infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. X-ray-photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a K-Alpha X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer System (ESCALAB 250, Thermo, USA), and C 1 s (284.8 eV) was used to calibrate the peak positions for the elemental analysis. Element analysis results were recorded using a Elementar Vario EL Cube Analyzer. The ESI-MS spectra were measured using Agilent Q-TOF 6510 mass spectrometer. The time resolved fluorescence spectra were measured using Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer.

A mixture of SiPcCl2 (0.3 mmol), 2-(2-aminoethoxy)ethanol (16.0 mmol), K2CO3 (200 mg) and pyridine (2 mL) was heated and refluxed at 130℃ in dry toluene (40 mL) for 12 h under nitrogen atmosphere. After evaporating the solvent in vacuo, the residue was redissolved in CHCl3 and washed three times with deionized water (100 mL). The crud product was recrystallized from chloroform/n-hexane to give SiPc-NH2 as a green solid (44 %). 1H NMR (CD3OD, 300 MHz): δ = 9.77–9.71 (8H, m, Pc-Hα), 8.52–8.43 (8H, m, Pc-Hβ), 1.92–1.87 (4H, t, CH2), 1.82 – 1.73 (4H, t, CH2), 0.59–0.56 (4H, t, CH2), −1.83 (4H, t, CH2).

Various metal ion chlorides (Cu2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+) and nitrate (Hg2+ and Ag+) were solubilized in deionized water to make a stock solution at the concentration of 1.25 mM. Then, different metal ion (5 equiv.) was added into SiPc-NH2 solution in THF (2 μM), followed by detection of absorbance and fluorescence changes. The fluorescence spectra were measured between 620–800 nm by excitation at 610 nm, and the fluorescence colorimetric changes were observed by a UV lamp at 365 nm. The metal-ion competition experiments were further conducted via adding CuCl2 (10 μM) into the solution of SiPc-NH2 (2 μM) with various metal ions (10 μM). All samples were determined in triplicate for a statistic purpose.

Various copper salts were solubilized in deionized water to make a stock solution at the concentration of 1.25 mM. Then 5 equivalents of copper salt (10 μM) was added into SiPc-NH2 (2 μM) THF solution. The fluorescence spectra were measured between 620–––800 nm by excitation at 610 nm, and the fluorescence colorimetric changes were observed by a UV lamp at 365 nm. The anion competition experiments were further conducted by adding CuCl2 (10 μM) into the solution of SiPc-NH2 (2 μM) with various anions (10 μM) (OAc-, Br-, ClO4 -, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, F-, HCO3 –, I-, OH–, S2-, HPO4 2-, NO3 –) and then colorimetric and fluorescence changes were detected subsequently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination binding stoichiometry of SiPc-NH2 to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The binding ratio between Cu2+and SiPc-NH2 was determined using titration of SiPc-NH2 (50 μM) solution by different concentration of CuCl2 solution with CuCl2/ SiPc-NH2 ratio from 0 to 2:1. Then, the colorimetric and fluorescence changes were monitored, followed by Job’s plot analyses, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray-photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Mass and elemental analyses measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cells culture and intracellular fluorescence detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) were used for biological evaluation. The cells were incubated in DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin and streptomycin (100 units mL−1, streptomycin 100 μg·mL−1, respectively) at 37 °C under 5 % CO2.

MCF-7 cells were seeded on a 24-well plate overnight for attachment and then incubated with SiPc-NH2 in medium (10 μM, 2.0 mL) for 4 h. After incubation, the cells were rinsed with PBS three times to remove excess SiPc-NH2. For the detection of Cu2+, the cells were further incubated with CuCl2 (30 μM) for 30 min. After cells were washed with PBS for three times, the fluorescence imaging was immediately performed on a confocal fluorescence micro-scope (Leica TCS SP8). The signals from the red channel (620–730 nm) were captured. The control group was incubated with only medium and obtained under the same conditions.

The configuration of a molecule is of great significance to its physical, chemical and biological properties, resulting in different applications [49]. Herein, SiPc-NH2 containing two flexible axial ligands with terminal –NH2 group was selected to probe metal ions through the trans- to cis-configuration interconversion after metal ion binding. To reveal structure–function relationship, 1H NMR was utilized to trace the cis/trans transformation of SiPc-NH2 in solution. Due to the paramagnetic effect of copper ion, the isomerization of SiPc-NH2 was carried out by polarity induced configuration change by H2O. The group signals of 1H NMR attributed to cis-SiPc-NH2 increased gradually after addition of D2O into the CDCl3 solution. We ascribe this transformation to the rearrangement of the two hydrophilic –NH2 groups, adopting a cis-SiPc-NH2 configuration to form J-aggregation nanoparticle with enhanced amphiphilicity [55]. At the same time, identical mass spectra pattern from both samples showed no substance changes occurred during transformation (Fig. 2 ). These results clearly verify the feasibility of metal ion induced configuration interconversion.

The metal ion selectivity of SiPc-NH2 was conducted by adding various metal ion chloride (Mn+ = Cu2+Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+) and nitrate (Hg2+ and Ag+) in deionized water into SiPc-NH2 solution (THF) to reach the final concentration of 2 μM and 10 μM for SiPc-NH2 and Mn+, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 a, b, both the fluorescence and visible color of SiPc-NH2 were remarkably turned off only in the presence of Cu2+. However, neither fluorescence nor visible color changes were noticed in the presence of other metal ions. The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra are exactly in accordance with these colorimetric observations (Fig. 3c–f). As the result, SiPc-NH2 has a sole selectivity for Cu2+ over the other metal ions. In order to investigate the possible interaction of other metal ion to the selectivity to Cu2+ by SiPc-NH2, metal-ion competition experiments were further conducted via adding equivalent concentration of CuCl2 (10 μM) into SiPc-NH2 (2 μM) with various metal ions (10 μM). As shown in Fig. 3g, the addition of excess amounts of other metal ions did not interfere with neither the binding of Cu2+ to SiPc-NH2 nor the subsequent fluorescence/color turn-off, further exhibit the high selectivity of sensor for Cu2+ over other metal ions. We attribute the instant fluorescence quenching to the heavy metal effect of Cu2+ towards SiPc-NH2. However, the absorption turn-off is caused by the precipitation of chelate from solution due to the polarity change after Cu2+ binding.

The concentration-dependent titration was carried out by varying the addition amount of CuCl2 (Fig. 4 ). After adding CuCl2, the fluorescence intensities decrease immediately along with the addition of CuCl2 (0–30 equiv.). In addition, a linear relationship could be observed between fluorescence F0/F and CuCl2 concentration, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after adding CuCl2 (Fig. 4c). This enables SiPc-NH2 a good fluorescence sensor for quantitively detection Cu2+. The detection limit for Cu2+ ions was calculated as 2.79 × 10-7 M based on the equation 3σ/S [56]. Due to the lagging of precipitation, the immediate response of absorption cannot be observed.

The selectivity of SiPc-NH2-Cu2+ towards anion was investigated by addition of different copper salts into the solution (Fig. 5 ). Both the visible color and fluorescence were turn-off only with the presence of anion Cl-, while no obviously changes in neither fluorescence nor absorption were observed with the presence of other anions. These indicated that SiPc-NH2-Cu2+ can induce color and fluorescence changes only with the presence of Cl-. Moreover, the anion Cl- selectivity was further measured by adding Cu(NO3)2 into the SiPc-NH2 solution at the presence of various anion. As shown in Fig. 6 , the color and fluorescence change only at the presence of Cl-, which again confirm the selectivity of SiPc-NH2-Cu2+ towards Cl-. The anion interfering effects were conducted by addition of CuCl2 (10 μM) into the mixture of SiPc-NH2 with various equivalent sodium salt (OAc-, Br-, ClO4 -, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, F-, HCO3 –, I-, HPO4 2-, NO3 –) (Figure S1). None of the selected anions significantly influenced the Cl- detection, indicating the satisfactory anti-interference of SiPc-NH2-Cu2+ towards Cl-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding mechanism of SiPc-NH2 to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Based on molecular structure of SiPc-NH2 and coordination properties of Cu2+, two reasonable binding models with stoichiometric ratio (1:1 or 2:1) between SiPc-NH2 and Cu2+ were shown in Fig. 7 . The binding stoichiometry ratio determination was first carried out by varying titration mole ratio of CuCl2/SiPc-NH2 from 0 to 2:1. The systematic titration changes in fluorescence and absorption are shown in Fig. 8 . Both the fluorescence and absorption are entirely turned off at a CuCl2/SiPc-NH2 molar ratio of 1:1, revealing a 1:1 stoichiometry existed between probe SiPc-NH2 and Cu2+. As we mentioned above, the instant fluorescence quenching is caused by heavy metal effect of Cu2+ and the absorption turn-off is attributed to the precipitation of chelate after binding. Actually, except DMF and DMSO, the resulting chelate is very stable and not soluble in most of organic and inorganic solvents, such as H2O, CHCl3, EtOH, CH3CN, THF, CH2Cl2 etc. (Figure S2); This is the preconditions for a heavy metal antidote to reduce toxicity and be removed from either environmental or biological systems. The coordination stoichiometry was further confirmed by elemental analysis of the chelate precipitate, which is in perfect agreement with the calculated data of the 1:1 binding stoichiometry (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ readout and removal in solution
ce:section.ce:para: By varying the counterpart ion in the system, dual-readout protocol for detection and removal Cu2+ in solution can be easily constructed. The detecting and removal of Cu2+ can be easily achieved by addition different Cu2+ salts into SiPc-NH2 solution with excess of NaCl (10 equiv.). As shown in Fig. 11 , the fluorescence and color of the sensor system changed remarkably after addition of Cu2+, such as CuSO4, Cu(NO3)2 and Cu(OAc)2 etc. This illustrates that the Cu2+ could be effectively detected and precipitate out under other interfering cation and anions in the system. Moreover, the existence of excess Cl- could accelerate the response to Cu2+ in the system.

On the other hand, by addition Cl- into SiPc-NH2 solution with excess of CuNO3 (10 equiv.), the sensing of Cl- can also be achieved by detection of either fluorescence or colorimetric responses. Both the fluorescence and color turned off after addition of Cl-, such as NaCl, KCl and FeCl3 etc. As the results, by variation the different components in the system, SiPc-NH2 can be used to sensing Cu2+ or Cl- separately. Moreover, the existence of excess Cu2+ could accelerate the response to Cl- in the system.

The fluorescence detecting of Cu2+ was conducted in MCF-7 cells that were incubated with pure medium, SiPc-NH2 or SiPc-NH2&CuCl2, as control, fluorescence or Cu2+ detecting group, respectively. As shown in Fig. 12 , the fluorescence of SiPc-NH2 in the detecting group was significantly reduced after incubation with the CuCl2, suggesting the intracellular formation of insoluble chelate for detection and removal Cu2+ in living cells. It worth noting, small molecule chelator is the most desirable agent in clinic for treatment heavy metal poison, which can form stable chelate with heavy metal ions to reduce their toxicity and excreted by urine or feces, such as prussian blue forms insoluble chelator with highly toxic thallium ion and excreted through intestine. As such, highly stable SiPc-NH2-CuCl2 can precipitate out from H2O along with colorimetric and fluorescent changes, which make it a multifunctional antidote candidate for both Cu2+ readout and removal in biological system.

In addition, the cell viabilities were monitored 24 h after treatment using MTT assay, and negligible dropping of cell viabilities were observed for SiPc-NH2&CuCl2 group with the Cu2+ concentration up to ∼ 45 μM (Fig. 12b). In contrast, the cell viabilities for the group incubated soly with Cu2+ dropped gradually along with increasing of concentrations, which further evidence the good biocompatibility of SiPc-NH2-CuCl2.

In summary, we have developed a novel multifunctional sensor systme with dual-mode NIR fluorescence and colorimetric readout for selective detection and removal of Cu2+ based on cis-/trans-configuration interconversion of silicon phthalocyanine. The binding mechanism and stoichiometry ratio of CuCl2/SiPc-NH2 (1:1) were systematically revealed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray-photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Mass spectra as well as elemental analyses of the resulting chelate. Competitive binding experiments in the presence of various anions and cations showed SiPc-NH2 exhibited extremely high anti-interference for recognition of Cu2+ and Cl-. Moreover, in vitro cell studies showed that Cu2+ could also be effectively detected in living cells and result in insoluble chelate for removal with good biocompatibility. In conclusion, this multifucntional NIR sensor is a novel small molecule antidote for either Cu2+ readout and removal in biological and complicated environmental systems.
Gaoqiang Ma: Investigation. Wei Zhang: Methodology. Wanru Zhao: Investigation. Guixue Lian: Investigation. Sen Zhang: Investigation. Dongjiao Zhang: Investigation. Wei Liu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Wei Liu reports financial support was provided by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province. Dongjiao Zhang reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2016BM16); National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82201101).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115355.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary figure 1
